Clinical Trial Updates

Clinical Trial Updates

Newly opened trials and enrollment.

Sort 3 articles
Clinical Trial Updates Phase 3

Phase 3: Subcutaneous vs IV pembrolizumab in first-line metastatic NSCLC (PD-L1 TPS ≥50%)

A randomized, open-label study evaluating the pharmacokinetics and safety of subcutaneous pembrolizumab co-formulated with hyaluronidase (MK-3475A) versus intravenous pembrolizumab.

Tumor type
Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
NCT ID
NCT06698042
Contact
Balazs Halmos · bahalmos@montefiore.org
Eligibility & details
Clinical Trial Updates Phase 1

Phase 1: QTX3034 in patients with KRAS-G12D-mutant advanced solid tumors

A trial evaluating the safety, tolerability, pharmacokinetics, and efficacy of QTX3034 in locally advanced/metastatic solid tumors with a KRAS-G12D mutation.

Tumor type
Locally Advanced / Metastatic Solid Tumors
NCT ID
NCT06227377
Contact
Fernand Bteich · fbteich@montefiore.org
Eligibility & details
Clinical Trial Updates Phase 3

Phase 3: Adjuvant pembrolizumab ± V940 in resectable stage II–IIIB NSCLC (INTerpath-009)

A randomized double-blind study in participants not achieving pathologic complete response after neoadjuvant pembrolizumab plus platinum-based doublet chemotherapy.

Tumor type
Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
NCT ID
NCT06623422
Contact
Brendon M. Stiles · bstiles@montefiore.org
Eligibility & details

Funding Opportunities

Grants and pilot awards for members.

Sort 3 articles
Funding Opportunities

WES (When Everyone Survives) Foundation Leukemia Research Grant

Supports new and established investigators pursuing laboratory, translational, or clinical research in acute leukemia.

Amount
$50,000
Term
One year
Deadline
June 15, 2025
Details
Funding Opportunities

MECCC Cancer Therapeutics Research Program Pilot Project

Supports innovative, collaborative basic and translational cancer research from multidisciplinary teams of two or more PIs.

Amount
$50,000 (no indirect costs)
Term
One year
Deadline
June 27, 2025 (award ~July 30, 2025)
Details
Funding Opportunities

Damon Runyon–Rachleff Innovation Award

Support for exceptionally creative early-career researchers with “high-risk/high-reward” ideas that could significantly impact cancer prevention, diagnosis, or treatment.

Amount
$400,000
Term
Two years
Deadline
July 1, 2025, 4pm EDT
Details

Spotlight

Spotlight

An in-depth look at MECCC programs.
SpotlightMay 22, 2025

The FAST Clinic: ensuring Bronx patients with lung nodules receive follow-up care

Since 2022, Montefiore Einstein's Follow-Up Assessment of Lung Nodules (FAST) Clinic has driven earlier lung-cancer detection — with stage-1 diagnoses rising from 25.7% to 31.5%.

Read more

Director Welcome

A Welcome from the MECCC Director

Draft — pending Dr. Steidl. Placeholder copy shown for layout only.

Office of the Director · May 27, 2025

This slot carries the Director's welcome that opens each issue. The copy below is placeholder text for the prototype only — the real welcome will be written in Dr. Ulrich Steidl's voice and approved before any launch.

Each issue, the Director's note frames the season's biggest milestones for Montefiore Einstein Comprehensive Cancer Center — leadership appointments, faculty honors, and the research and community work advancing our mission to reduce the burden of cancer in the Bronx.

We are #MECCCPROUD of our members and grateful to the patients and community partners who make this work possible.

Share LinkedIn Email
  1. Home
  2. MECCC News
  3. Multi-omics and Exposome Mini Symposium draws gl…

MECCC News

Multi-omics and Exposome Mini Symposium draws global leaders to the Bronx

More than 40 MECCC faculty, fellows, students and staff explored the “missing link between genes and health” at a Cancer Epidemiology, Prevention & Control program symposium.

May 20, 2025

Over forty MECCC faculty, fellows, students and staff attended MECCC's interactive Cancer Epidemiology, Prevention & Control Program Mini Symposium on “Multi-omics and Exposome Approaches in Cancer Research” at the Price Center/Block Research Pavilion on May 20, 2025.

“With us today are three of the global leaders in the field of multi-omics and exposome research,” said MECCC's H. Dean Hosgood, PhD, MPH, Professor in the Department of Epidemiology and Population Health at Albert Einstein College of Medicine and Co-Leader of MECCC's Cancer Epidemiology, Prevention & Control Research Program. “We've left time for Q&A after each talk — because there is a lot to learn and discuss with these experts about how we might apply their research methods, insights, and real-world applications here in the Bronx.”

Sometimes called the “missing link between genes and health,” the exposome — the culminating biological mechanisms by which genetics, environmental exposures and social needs may influence cancer outcomes — is an area Dr. Hosgood began focusing on during the pandemic. But it's “so insanely complex,” he described thinking to himself. “How do we begin researching the exposome in the Bronx?”

He started by asking what the community wanted, reaching out to the 20-plus members of MECCC's Community Advisory Board and attendees at MECCC Town Halls for input on what they and their families are concerned about regarding the environment they live in day to day. Answers ranged from air quality to housing insecurity, transportation to healthcare appointments, daily stress, and more. Based on this feedback, Dr. Hosgood initiated exposome research in the Bronx by focusing on the concerns most voiced by the community. In his talk, “Quantifying the Exposome of the Bronx for Health Outcomes Research,” he shared MECCC research findings to date.

Guest speakers provided framework for another question Dr. Hosgood posed: “What's next for exposome research in the Bronx?” Roel Vermeulen, PhD (Utrecht University) presented “How Can Exposomics be Used to Better Understand Cancer Etiology, Prediction and Prognosis?”; Marc Chadeau, PhD (Imperial College London) presented “A Multi-Omic Analysis of Smoking-Induced Carcinogens: A Stability Selection Approach”; and George Downward, PhD (Utrecht University) presented “Practical Application of Exposomic Surfaces for Lung Cancer Risk: Experiences from the NELSON Projects.”

Among the topics discussed: building “exposure risk scores” for cancers and other illnesses, better understanding contributions from the “internal exposome,” integrating individual susceptibility into prediction models, and clarifying which features to include in data modeling so more reliable screening recommendations can be explored.

Share LinkedIn Email

  3. Britta Will, PhD, named director of Einstein's G…

MECCC News

Britta Will, PhD, named director of Einstein's Gottesman Institute for Stem Cell Biology

A senior MECCC member and Co-Leader of the Stem Cell & Cancer Biology Program, Dr. Will takes the helm of the Gottesman Institute from interim director Dr. Ulrich Steidl.

May 19, 2025

Montefiore Einstein has named Britta Will, PhD, the new director of Albert Einstein College of Medicine's Ruth L. and David S. Gottesman Institute for Stem Cell Biology and Regenerative Medicine. Dr. Will — Associate Professor of Cell Biology and Oncology and the Diane and Arthur B. Belfer Faculty Scholar in Cancer Research — takes over the role from Ulrich Steidl, MD, PhD, who had served as interim director since 2021.

A senior MECCC member, Dr. Will serves as Co-Leader of the Stem Cell & Cancer Biology Research Program and Director of Preclinical Modeling for the Blood Cancer Institute. She emerged as the clear choice to serve as the Gottesman Institute's permanent director following an extensive nationwide search, and succeeds the institute's late founding director, Dr. Paul Frenette.

In addition to years of productive scientific excellence in her own NIH-funded laboratory, Dr. Will has demonstrated outstanding leadership and a deep commitment to mentoring and training the next generation of scientists. Congratulations, Dr. Will — we are #MECCCPROUD!

Honors, Awards & Achievements

Congratulations to our first round of 2025 Pilot Project awardees

MECCC leadership announces the teams awarded one-year Cancer Epidemiology, Prevention & Control and Cancer Center Support Grant Team Science pilot grants.

May 15, 2025

MECCC leadership announces funding opportunities for members and the larger community throughout the year, in support of MECCC's mission to reduce the burden of cancer in the Bronx through cancer-focused research, education and training, clinical care, and community outreach.

The following teams were awarded one-year Cancer Epidemiology, Prevention & Control Pilot Project grants beginning May 2025: Haiyeng Cheng, MD, PhD, John C. McAuliffe, MD, PhD, Chaoyuang Kuang, MD, PhD, and Xiaonan Xue, PhD — “From Imaging to Action: AI-Assessed Body Composition and Pancreatic Cancer Outcomes”; and Richard S. Ha, MD, Sheldon M. Feldman, MD, and Michael Z. Liu, PhD — “AI-Based Imaging Biomarker for Enhanced Breast Cancer Risk Stratification and Optimized Patient Selection in Supplemental MRI Screening.”

The following teams were awarded Cancer Center Support Grant Team Science Pilot Project grants beginning in May and June: Kamini Singh, PhD, Xingxing Zang, PhD, John C. McAuliffe, MD, PhD, and Simone Sidoli, PhD — “Exploring Novel Immune Epitopes and T-Cell Therapy for Pancreatic Cancer”; Charles B. Hall, PhD, Rachel Zeig-Owens, DrPH, H. Dean Hosgood, PhD, and Amit K. Verma, MD — “Cancer and Cognition in a Diverse Urban Cohort”; and David Shechter, PhD, Seiya Kitamura, PhD, and Kira Gritsman, MD, PhD — “Targeting TTLL4 as a Novel Vulnerability in NPM1c-mutant Acute Myeloid Leukemia.”

Honors, Awards & Achievements

Dr. Steidl honored with 14th Annual Marshall S. Horwitz, MD, Faculty Prize for Research Excellence

MECCC Deputy Director Ulrich G. Steidl, MD, PhD, delivered the keynote lecture at a May 6 ceremony on the Einstein campus.

May 6, 2025

Ulrich G. Steidl, MD, PhD, Professor and Chair of Cell Biology at Albert Einstein College of Medicine and Deputy Director of MECCC, was honored with the Marshall S. Horwitz, MD, Faculty Prize for Research Excellence at a ceremony on the Einstein campus on May 6.

Dr. Steidl, who also holds the title of Edward P. Evans Endowed Professor for Myelodysplastic Syndromes and Co-Director of MECCC's Blood Cancer Institute, delivered the keynote lecture, “Stem Cell Heterogeneity in Normal and Malignant Hematopoiesis from Discovery to Clinical Implications.” His research has significantly advanced understanding of cancers affecting blood-forming cells, including leukemia, lymphoma, and myelodysplastic syndromes.

The prize honors the late Dr. Horwitz, an internationally renowned scientist known for his study of the biology of adenoviruses and a member of the Einstein faculty for nearly 40 years.

Honors, Awards & Achievements

Dr. Aguirre-Ghiso receives University of Colorado Comprehensive Cancer Center “Cancer Warrior Award”

The award recognizes outstanding contributions to cancer research and devotion to training the next generation.

May 2, 2025

Julio Aguirre-Ghiso, PhD, Professor and Rose C. Falkenstein Chair in Cancer Research at Albert Einstein College of Medicine, Co-Leader of MECCC's Tumor Microenvironment & Metastasis Research Program, and Director of MECCC's Cancer Dormancy Institute, received the award in recognition of his “outstanding contributions and achievements in cancer research and devotion to training the next generation of cancer warriors.” He was presented the award after delivering a lecture at the University of Colorado Comprehensive Cancer Center in February.

“Totally honored to receive this award from the hands of the amazing James DeGregori, PhD,” said Dr. Aguirre-Ghiso. “This award is for past and present members of my lab; without them it would not have been possible.”

Conference News

MECCC breast-surgery research featured at the American Society of Breast Surgeons Annual Meeting

MECCC physicians, fellows, and staff presented a “Quick Shot” talk and six poster abstracts in Las Vegas.

May 4, 2025

MECCC physicians, fellows, and staff working in breast cancer surgery research presented recent findings through talks and poster sessions at the American Society of Breast Surgeons Annual Meeting in Las Vegas, NV.

The “Quick Shot” presenter was Priyanka Parmar, MD, a Postdoctoral Fellow in the Department of Surgery, who spoke on “Neighborhood-Level Disadvantage as a Predictor of Metastatic Breast Cancer at Diagnosis and Delayed Treatment Initiation,” conducted with colleagues including Jessica Lin, Fardeen Bhimani, Susan Laura Jao, Michelle Sheckley, and others.

Poster abstracts included work by Michelle Sheckley, MD (“Palpable Breast Cancer among Patients in the Bronx is Associated with Inadequate Screening and Later-Stage Diagnosis”); Susan Laura Jao, MD (bioimpedance spectroscopy and lymphatic-sparing sentinel lymph node biopsy); Abigail Brooks, MD (“Intraoperative Radiation (IORT) for Breast Cancer: Does One Size Fit All?”); Dr. Parmar (Medicaid expansion and mastectomy choice analyses); and Sheng Chow, MD.

Conference News

MECCC at AACR 2025: a press-release-highlighted study on cancer-related fatigue and QoL

Simo Du, MD, MHS, had a poster on sex disparities in cancer-related fatigue and depression selected for an AACR press release.

April 30, 2025

Several Montefiore Einstein faculty, fellows, and students attended this year's AACR Annual Meeting in Chicago from April 25 to 30. Among them was Simo Du, MD, MHS, with NYC Health + Hospitals/Jacobi and Albert Einstein College of Medicine, whose poster abstract, “Sex Disparities in Cancer-Related Fatigue, Depression, and Impact on Quality of Life among Cancer Survivors: Insights from NHANES,” was selected for highlighting in an AACR press release.

The research — conducted in collaboration with colleagues from Einstein, Montefiore, and Mount Sinai Hospital, and with Dr. Du's mentor, MECCC member Jesus D. Anampa Mesias, MD — described a nearly twofold reduction in recreational physical activity and quality of life among cancer survivors due to cancer-related fatigue and depression, especially among female cancer survivors. The results were based on self-reported findings from National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) data from the 2015–2016 and 2017–2020 cycles.

Among all cancer survivors, those who reported cancer-related fatigue were 86% more likely to reduce their amount of moderate recreational activities such as brisk walking, leisurely bicycling, golfing, and light gardening. Those who reported feeling depressed were 65% more likely to reduce both moderate recreational activities and vigorous activities such as jogging, cross-country skiing, and hiking steep terrain. Females were 69% more likely to experience cancer-related fatigue and 58% more likely to experience depression than male survivors.

“Our findings highlight the importance of providing special attention and tailored interventions such as exercise programs, support groups, and mind-body behavioral techniques for vulnerable groups to help effectively manage fatigue and improve participation in recreational activities, as they are an essential aspect of quality of life,” said Dr. Du. She noted that she hopes future studies can evaluate tailored interventions and the connection between cancer-related fatigue and different mechanisms, such as inflammatory markers, to see if gender modifies the association.

Montefiore Einstein poster abstracts at AACR this year also included work from En Cheng, MD, PhD (AI chatbots and their responses to Spanish-language cancer questions); Simo Du, MD, MHS (inpatient outcomes and treatment burdens among CAR-T recipients); Mark Forsberg, MD (LSD1 and BCL-XL inhibitors in JAK2+ AML/MPN cells); Akansha Jalota, PhD and Tanya Verma (cigarette smoking and prognosis in myelodysplastic syndromes); Rahul Sanawar, PhD (monoclonal antibodies against pathogenic S100A8/9 in myelodysplasia and leukemia models); and Yuqing Wang, MD, PhD (large language models for simplifying oncology terminology in patient education).

Share LinkedIn Email

Conference News

Dr. Gavathiotis presents allosteric drug-discovery work at Drug Discovery Chemistry

MECCC's Cancer Therapeutics co-leader spoke on targeting BCL-2 family proteins in San Diego.

April 17, 2025

At this year's Drug Discovery Chemistry conference in San Diego (April 14–17), Evris Gavathiotis, PhD, Professor of Biochemistry at Albert Einstein College of Medicine and Co-Leader of MECCC's Cancer Therapeutics Research Program, spoke as part of “Innovative Approaches for Difficult Targets” within the Protein-Protein Interactions track.

In his presentation, “A Platform for Allosteric Drug Discovery Targeting Protein-Protein Interactions: Focus on BCL-2 Family Proteins,” Dr. Gavathiotis presented an integrated computational and experimental approach for identifying allosteric sites and inhibitors — showcasing the discovery of allosteric inhibitors targeting the anti-apoptotic protein BCL-XL, a novel strategy to modulate apoptosis across cancers.

Conference News

MECCC nurses present “Essentials of Oncology Course” at the 50th ONS Congress

A nursing-education initiative to build confidence among novice oncology nurses, presented in Denver.

April 13, 2025

MECCC nurses Christina Osorio, Susan Sakalian, Arlene Simpson, and Carolyn Ellis presented “Essentials of Oncology Course: A Nursing Education Initiative to Increase Confidence among Novice Oncology Nurses” at the 50th Annual Oncology Nursing Society Congress, April 9–13 in Denver, Colorado.

The course supports and empowers early-career oncology nurses and is part of a larger effort to enhance education for MECCC nurses and expand the nursing “gold standard” of Magnet status to all patient-care facilities.

Funding Opportunities

WES (When Everyone Survives) Foundation Leukemia Research Grant

Supports new and established investigators pursuing laboratory, translational, or clinical research in acute leukemia.

May 12, 2025

Purpose: To encourage and support new and established investigators to pursue laboratory, translational, or clinical research related to acute leukemia. WES specializes in searching for more- as well as less-harsh treatment options for patients with this cancer.

Amount: $50,000. Term: One year. Deadline: June 15, 2025.

Contact Jamel Thomas (jamel.thomas@einsteinmed.edu) and Gillian Daronzio (gillian.grandy@einsteinmed.edu), or Audrey McInerney (amcinerney@montefiore.org), for information or assistance.

Amount

$50,000

Term

One year

Deadline

June 15, 2025

Contact

Jamel Thomas / Gillian Daronzio / Audrey McInerney

Funding Opportunities

MECCC Cancer Therapeutics Research Program Pilot Project

Supports innovative, collaborative basic and translational cancer research from multidisciplinary teams of two or more PIs.

May 12, 2025

Purpose: To support innovative and collaborative basic and translational cancer research from multidisciplinary teams of two or more Principal Investigators, encouraging intra- and inter-programmatic collaborations leading to drug development and novel approaches for cancer treatment. Preference is given to applications relevant to MECCC's catchment area (lung, prostate, multiple myeloma, breast, liver, colorectal cancer; leukemia). Funding is available for two research projects.

Amount: $50,000 (no indirect costs). Term: One year. Deadline: June 27, 2025; anticipated award date July 30, 2025.

For scientific-relevance questions, contact Steve Almo, Evris Gavathiotis, or Yvonne Saenger. To apply, contact Jamel Thomas and Cecilia Lopez for materials.

Amount

$50,000 (no indirect costs)

Term

One year

Deadline

June 27, 2025 (award ~July 30, 2025)

Contact

Jamel Thomas / Cecilia Lopez

Funding Opportunities

Damon Runyon–Rachleff Innovation Award

Support for exceptionally creative early-career researchers with “high-risk/high-reward” ideas that could significantly impact cancer prevention, diagnosis, or treatment.

May 12, 2025

Purpose: To provide support for the next generation of exceptionally creative thinkers with “high-risk/high-reward” ideas. The Innovation Award funds extraordinary early-career researchers who have an innovative idea but lack sufficient preliminary data to obtain traditional funding. It is not designed to fund incremental advances; applicants with backgrounds in multiple disciplines are especially encouraged to apply.

Amount: $400,000. Term: Two years. Deadline: July 1, 2025, 4pm EDT.

In addition to the opportunities above, check the Montefiore Einstein Office of Grant Support website and its SPINPlus portal for targeted funding opportunities.

Amount

$400,000

Term

Two years

Deadline

July 1, 2025, 4pm EDT

Contact

Jamel Thomas / Gillian Daronzio / Audrey McInerney

Spotlight

The FAST Clinic: ensuring Bronx patients with lung nodules receive follow-up care

Since 2022, Montefiore Einstein's Follow-Up Assessment of Lung Nodules (FAST) Clinic has driven earlier lung-cancer detection — with stage-1 diagnoses rising from 25.7% to 31.5%.

May 22, 2025

Since launching in 2022, Montefiore Einstein's Follow-Up Assessment of Lung Nodules (FAST) Clinic at MECCC has had a tremendous impact in ensuring that patients with lung nodules in the Bronx quickly receive appropriate follow-up care.

“Bronx residents continue to be diagnosed with lung cancer at more advanced, less curable stages than people in other places in the country,” explains FAST Clinic leader Neel P. Chudgar, MD, a thoracic surgeon and Associate Director of Clinical and Translational Research in the Department of Thoracic Surgery. “And while we've had some success with lung cancer screening, challenges persist — including lack of awareness, fears and stigma, access challenges, and cost concerns.”

“But with our FAST Clinic, we are increasingly identifying people with early-stage lung cancers by catching and examining suspicious lung nodules and thoracic abnormalities,” he says. “And we are now seeing fewer lung cancer cases at a late stage of the disease, when treatment options have diminished.”

These developments bode well for residents of the Bronx, says Brendon Stiles, MD, Chief of the Divisions of Thoracic Surgery and Surgical Oncology, MECCC Associate Director for Surgical Oncology, and a member of the FAST Clinic. “The biggest impact on lung cancer outcomes likely lies in early detection, when the disease is much more curable.”

The FAST Clinic originated with a Health Equity and Inclusiveness Junior Investigator Award to Dr. Chudgar from the LUNGevity Foundation in 2021. Run by Montefiore Einstein thoracic surgeons and interventional pulmonologists, the clinic has received more than 1,000 referrals and diagnosed over 80 cancers.

Not only has early-stage detection increased — with the rate of stage-1 diagnoses at Montefiore Einstein rising from 25.7% to 31.5% — but there has been nearly a 12% reduction in the incidence of advanced stage IV lung cancers.

Additional factors contributing to the clinic's success include access to the new robotic bronchoscopy suite at Weiler Hospital, which complements the suite at the Moses campus. The technology enables more accurate biopsies of small lung nodules through minimally invasive techniques, with real-time 2D and 3D visualization. The clinic recently reached a milestone of 1,000 robotic bronchoscopies completed, and there has been a two- to three-fold increase in surgical volume.

FAST Clinic interventional pulmonologist and Assistant Professor Hyun S. Kim, MD, explains that the clinic “can now accurately access nodules as small as 4 to 6mm, leading to increased early-stage cancer detection. Our diagnostic accuracy has improved from the 70%–80% range to the 90s — well above the national average.”

Another key to the clinic's effectiveness: same-day diagnosis and treatment for select patients. Using fluorescent markers during robotic bronchoscopy, surgeons can precisely locate and remove small lesions robotically in a single procedure — reducing treatment timelines and simplifying access to care. Early detection also makes lung-sparing surgery more likely.

Colleagues can raise the tide of referrals to the FAST Clinic through an “ambulatory referral to lung nodule clinic” in EPIC, or by emailing lungnodule@montefiore.org.

Share LinkedIn Email

  2. In Our Community
  3. MECCC welcomes a new cohort of Bronx Oncology Li…

In Our Community

MECCC welcomes a new cohort of Bronx Oncology Living Daily (BOLD) interns

Seven undergraduate, postgraduate, and gap-year students join a competitive one-year program in patient navigation, community outreach, and research.

May 25, 2025

MECCC is excited to greet another cohort of Bronx Oncology Living Daily (BOLD) one-year interns. This group of seven undergraduate, postgraduate, and gap-year students — from schools such as Hunter College, Fordham University, and Long Island University — was selected following a competitive application process.

These aspiring future medical students, researchers, and healthcare providers join two other cycles of annual BOLD intern cohorts, and can look forward to invaluable, hands-on experience in patient navigation, community outreach, psycho-oncology program operations, and research projects.

The BOLD program reflects MECCC's investment in the Bronx community and in developing the next generation of compassionate cancer-care leaders. It complements the cancer center's broader community engagement, including a 20-plus-member Community Advisory Board and regular MECCC Town Halls that shape research priorities such as the Bronx exposome work.

Professional Development

CME: “Updates in Screening/Management of Ovarian Cancer”

A panel including Mark H. Einstein, MD, and Subuhee Hussain, MD, on novel approaches including neoadjuvant therapy and HIPEC.

September 5, 2025

A panel of experts — including Montefiore Einstein Chella & Moise Safra Endowed Chair and Department Chair Mark H. Einstein, MD, and White Plains Hospital Section Chief for Medical Oncology Subuhee Hussain, MD — will discuss novel approaches to ovarian cancer management, including shifts to neoadjuvant therapy and surgical interventions such as HIPEC.

The event is designed for medical oncologists, gynecologic oncologists, general gynecologists, and primary care physicians.

Event details

  • September 5, 2025 · 7am–1pm
  • Kanopi Restaurant, Opus Hotel, White Plains, NY
  • Contact Anne McDarby (amcdarby@montefiore.org) for details

Professional Development

MECCC Tumor Boards — earn CME credits

Meeting dates and times vary across MECCC's 19 tumor boards; attendees may receive 1.0–2.0 CME credits.

May 1, 2025

Meeting dates and times vary for MECCC's 19 tumor boards. Anyone who attends may receive CME credits (1.0–2.0) but must first email a request to the course director or coordinator, then register, receive a passcode, and complete an evaluation.

Special invitation to subscribe: MECCC invites everyone interested in receiving news from the cancer center to subscribe to the newsletter and share it with colleagues, students, and staff.
